nativeRange.habitat: Single geothermal cavern pool (Devil’s Hole, Amargosa Desert, Nye County, Nevada) — a collapsed limestone fault feature with near-constant 33°C water, extremely low dissolved oxygen, and a single shallow rock shelf (~3.5 m²) used for foraging and spawning. Water source is ancient groundwater with no surface inlet or outlet.

decorPreferences: Not applicable — cannot be legally kept in private aquaria
Conservation facility: shallow flat rock shelf replicating natural foraging ledge
Minimal decor; low-oxygen, warm geothermal water conditions

diet.notes: In the wild, subsists almost entirely on algae, diatoms, ostracods, and organic detritus deposited on the single shallow rock shelf. Extremely food-scarce environment; the species has evolved the remarkable ability to produce viable fry without a yolk-sac larval stage — an adaptation unique among vertebrates to its near-zero food availability. Conservation facility diet includes algae-based flake, cultured diatoms, and live invertebrates.

avoidWith: Any other species — wild population exists in complete isolation; not mixed with other fish under any circumstances

imageCandidates.notes: US FWS photo of male Cyprinodon diabolis showing vivid blue iridescence. Widely reproduced in species coverage.

summary: The world’s most range-restricted vertebrate — a tiny (2.5 cm) vivid-blue pupfish found only in a single geothermal cave pool in Nevada. Critically Endangered; US Endangered Species Act listed since 1967. Population fluctuates ~30–500 individuals seasonally. Not available to private hobbyists under any circumstances.
careNotes: Cyprinodon diabolis is the world’s most geographically restricted fish and one of the rarest vertebrates on Earth. Its entire wild population inhabits Devil’s Hole, a geothermal cavern within Devil’s Hole National Monument (Death Valley National Park), Nevada — a single fault-fed pool whose only productive surface measures roughly 3.5 m². Wild population counts from NPS/USFWS monitoring have ranged from approximately 35 individuals (2007 survey low) to around 500 in favorable years, with seasonal fluctuations driven by algae production on the shallow shelf. The species was among the first listed under the US Endangered Species Act (1967) and is protected under federal law. All captive specimens are held exclusively at the Ash Meadows Fish Conservation Facility (AMFCF), operated by the US Fish & Wildlife Service adjacent to the Amargosa Valley, Nevada, as a genetic and demographic backup population. Private possession is illegal in the United States and effectively impossible globally. Water temperature in Devil’s Hole remains near-constant at approximately 33°C year-round; dissolved oxygen is exceptionally low by aquarium standards (~2–3 mg/L). The species has evolved an extraordinary reproductive adaptation: fry are born without a yolk sac, capable of feeding immediately — believed to be unique among vertebrates and interpreted as an extreme response to the near-absence of food reserves in the cave. Males are iridescent vivid blue; females are olive-yellow. Isolation since the last glacial period (~10,000 years ago) has produced a genetically distinct population with extremely low genetic diversity. This entry is provided for visitor reference and conservation awareness only.
breedingNotes: Breeding occurs on the single submerged rock shelf in Devil’s Hole. Unique among all known vertebrates: fry hatch without a yolk-sac stage and must feed immediately after birth — an extreme adaptation to the food-scarce cave environment. At the Ash Meadows Fish Conservation Facility, captive propagation uses artificial shelves that replicate the shallow-ledge spawning substrate. Breeding is not possible in private aquaria; the AMFCF program is the sole conservation breeding effort.

primary.name: FishBase — Cyprinodon diabolis
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (max 2.5 cm TL), temperature range (32–34°C), native range (Devil’s Hole, Nevada, USA), taxonomy (Cyprinodontidae, Cyprinodontiformes), habitat (geothermal cave pool), diet (algae, diatoms, ostracods), and ESA listing status.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Cyprinodon diabolis
additional.notes: Confirmed water parameters (temp 32–34°C, pH 7.2–7.5), wild habitat description, population status, ESA listing history (1967), Ash Meadows Fish Conservation Facility as sole captive-holding institution, and yolk-sac-free fry adaptation detail.

additional.name: National Park Service — Devil’s Hole Species Profile
additional.notes: Provided wild population monitoring context (seasonal range ~35–500 individuals), description of Devil’s Hole as a geothermal cavern with ~3.5 m² productive rock shelf, depth structure, and federal protection status under the ESA and National Monument designation.

additional.name: US Fish & Wildlife Service — Ash Meadows Fish Conservation Facility
additional.notes: Described the captive backup program at AMFCF, artificial spawning shelf design, isolation protocols, and legal prohibition on private possession. Confirmed the yolk-sac-free fry characteristic as biologically documented.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Devil’s Hole Pupfish
additional.notes: Conservation-focused trade-press coverage. Confirmed hobbyist prohibition, wild population counts, evolutionary isolation timeline (~10,000 years post-glacial), vivid blue male coloration, and AMFCF as only authorised captive facility.

fish.breedingNotes: Cannot be bred in private aquaria; captive propagation exists solely at the US Fish & Wildlife Service Ash Meadows Fish Conservation Facility. Uniquely among vertebrates, fry are born without a yolk sac and must feed immediately — an extreme adaptation to food scarcity in Devil’s Hole.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: One of the world’s rarest fish (~100–500 wild individuals). US Endangered Species Act listed (1967). Found only in Devil’s Hole, Death Valley NV. Captive backup population maintained ONLY at US Fish & Wildlife Service Ash Meadows Fish Conservation Facility — NEVER in private hands. Included here for visitor reference only; cannot be legally kept.
